label: fiction
title: The Lighthouse Keepers
charge: Murder of a fellow lighthouse keeper
elements: The keeper's death was caused by another's act.

beats.text: The relief crew describe what they found after five weeks of storm: the survivor wild-eyed and whispering to himself, and his colleague's body lashed upright to the gallery rail, weeks dead.
beats.reveal_note: Horror, honestly reported — and read exactly backwards. A murderer has the whole sea for a grave; only an innocent man needs the body seen.

beats.text: The official light log, entered in evidence: it stops three days before the death and never resumes.
beats.reveal_note: The log drowned with the lantern-room door — the soaked pages were in evidence too. He kept the record on a slate after that, and the relief crew wiped it clean before anyone thought to copy it.

beats.text: The surgeon's findings: injuries consistent with a fall from the lamp gallery to the rocks — and rope-burns across the dead man's own palms, days old, from line run out fast.
beats.reveal_note: The dead man's hands tell it: he was working the vane line when the gust took him over. Fists leave marks on a beaten man; a fall from a working gallery leaves these.

beats.text: The shore station's weather log: five unbroken weeks in which no boat could land at the rock — and a passing brig's note that the light's distress signal was correctly flown throughout.
beats.reveal_note: He did everything a man alone on a rock could do: flew the signal, kept the light, and kept his friend where the relief would find him. The weather, not guilt, wrote the delay.

beats.text: The judge: what five weeks alone with the dead does to a man is a matter for pity, not proof. The jury must convict on evidence of an act — not on the state they found him in.
beats.reveal_note: The caution the whole case turned on: his broken mind was the sea's work; the charge required it to be his hands'.

twist: He lashed his friend's body where the relief would see it for the same reason he flew the signal: so no one could say the sea had swallowed a secret. The surgeon read a fall — and the vane line — in the dead man's palms; the shore log proved no boat could land for five weeks. The man they carried off the rock was guilty of nothing but surviving it.
